threats.title: transferFrom Fee Deduction Bypasses ERC20 Allowance, Draining Sender Beyond Approval
threats.hypothesis_description: _payTxFee deducts transaction fees directly from the sender's balance via _update() without consuming any allowance. Then super.transferFrom() only checks and consumes allowance for the transfer amount. Total tokens deducted from sender = amount + fees, but allowance only covers amount. Any approved spender can force the sender to lose more tokens than approved.
threats.investigation_rationale: This violates the core ERC20 allowance invariant. At 100% fee rate (allowed by setTxFeeRate), an approval of N tokens lets the spender extract 2N tokens from the sender. Even at normal fee rates, every transferFrom extracts extra tokens beyond what the sender authorized.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _payTxFee calls _update(from, _feesFaucet, txFees) which bypasses allowance; then super.transferFrom only deducts 'amount' from allowance

preconditions: Sender has approved spender for some amount
_txfeeRate > 0 (non-zero fee rate configured by ADMIN)
_feesFaucet != address(0)
Sender has sufficient balance for amount + fees

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: _update(from, _feesFaucet, txFees) deducts fees without allowance check; super.transferFrom only consumes 'amount' from allowance
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Total tokens removed from sender (amount + txFees) exceeds the allowance approved by sender
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to include fees in allowance consumption or check total deduction against allowance

analysis_questions: Can a spender repeatedly call transferFrom to drain sender beyond initial approval?
What is the maximum extractable amount at various fee rates?
Does this affect DeFi integrations that rely on standard ERC20 allowance semantics?
validation_requirements: Trace exact balance and allowance changes through transferFrom with non-zero fee
Calculate maximum extraction: at 100% fee, approval of X allows extracting 2X from sender
Test with standard DeFi patterns (approve + transferFrom)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Sender approves spender for 1000 EURF. Fee rate is 1% (100 bps). Spender calls transferFrom(sender, recipient, 1000). _payTxFee deducts 10 EURF fee from sender (no allowance check). super.transferFrom transfers 1000 EURF from sender and consumes 1000 from allowance. Sender loses 1010 EURF total but only approved 1000. At 100% fee rate, spender extracts 2000 EURF with a 1000 approval.
prerequisites: Non-zero fee rate must be set
Any user with any ERC20 approval can exploit

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Every transferFrom extracts extra tokens beyond approval. At maximum fee rate (100%), impact doubles the approved amount. Breaks all DeFi integrations relying on allowance semantics.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who have granted any ERC20 approvals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix; extracted tokens are in feesFaucet

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for total deduction vs allowance comparison in fee-bearing transferFrom

threats.title: Forwarder Accepts Arbitrary Domain Separator Enabling Cross-Contract/Cross-Chain Signature Replay
threats.hypothesis_description: The Forwarder.execute() accepts domainSeparator as an external parameter and passes it directly to _verifySig() without validating it against the Forwarder's own contract address or chain. Any valid EIP-712 signature from the user on any contract with the same ForwardRequest struct layout can be replayed on this Forwarder by passing the original contract's domain separator.
threats.investigation_rationale: EIP-712 domain separators exist specifically to prevent cross-contract replay. By accepting arbitrary domain separators, this protection is entirely bypassed. The project targets Ethereum and Polygon, making cross-chain replay a concrete threat for multi-chain users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: domainSeparator parameter used directly in digest computation without validation against contract's own domain; Forwarder has no EIP-712 domain separator to compare against

preconditions: User has signed a ForwardRequest on another Forwarder instance or chain with matching struct format
Target EURF address matches req.to on the target Forwarder
User nonce on target Forwarder matches the signed nonce (e.g., both 0 for new users)
User has sufficient balance on target chain for transfer + fees

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: bytes32 digest = keccak256(abi.encodePacked("\x19\x01", domainSeparator, ...)) uses caller-supplied domainSeparator
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No comparison of domainSeparator against a contract-derived value; Forwarder does not inherit EIP712 and has no own domain separator
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing: require(domainSeparator == _computeDomainSeparator()) or inheriting EIP-712 for domain binding

analysis_questions: Does the project deploy Forwarders at the same address on Ethereum and Polygon?
Can signatures from OpenZeppelin's MinimalForwarder (same struct) be replayed here?
What is the nonce state of users on secondary Forwarder deployments?
validation_requirements: Verify Forwarder has no internal domain separator computation
Test cross-chain replay scenario with matching EURF addresses
Test replay from a different Forwarder instance on same chain

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User signs ForwardRequest on Polygon Forwarder (nonce=0, to=EURF_polygon, transfer 1000 EURF to recipient). Same user has never used Ethereum Forwarder (nonce=0). If EURF addresses match across chains, attacker submits the same signature to Ethereum Forwarder with Polygon's domain separator. Signature verifies because domainSeparator is unchecked. Transfer of 1000 EURF executes on Ethereum without user intent.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unauthorized transfers of user tokens on secondary chain/Forwarder. Full balance at risk per replayed transaction.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Tokens transferred to attacker-controlled recipient are unrecoverable without forceTransfer

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for cross-contract or cross-chain signature replay with different domain separators

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction, after obtaining signature from another context
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Replay detectable by monitoring nonce consumption on multiple Forwarders

threats.title: Revoked Controller Can Re-Enable Safety Switch Despite Role Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: The safetySwitch re-enable path checks _operatingController == _msgSender() but does NOT verify the caller still holds the CONTROLLER role. A controller who activated the switch and was subsequently removed from the CONTROLLER role retains the ability to re-enable operations, undermining the emergency halt mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a window where a compromised controller that was deactivated can still undo the emergency shutdown, allowing malicious minting/burning to resume. The only other re-enable path requires DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Re-enable branch: if (!hasRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, _msgSender()) && _operatingController != _msgSender()) revert; Missing: && hasRole(CONTROLLER, _msgSender()) check

preconditions: Controller C activated safetySwitch (set _operating=false, _operatingController=C)
Owner removed C from CONTROLLER role via removeController(C)
C still has access to their private key

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Revoked controller address is stored in _operatingController and can still pass the re-enable check
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing hasRole(CONTROLLER, _msgSender()) requirement in re-enable path

analysis_questions: Can the owner preempt this by calling safetySwitch themselves to re-enable and clear _operatingController?
Is there a way to clear _operatingController without re-enabling operations?
Can the compromised controller re-enable and then immediately trigger malicious minting?
validation_requirements: Verify removeController does not clear _operatingController
Test: controller activates switch → owner removes controller → removed controller calls safetySwitch
Confirm DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE can also re-enable (as alternative remediation path)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Controller C is compromised and activates safetySwitch to halt operations as cover. Owner removes C's CONTROLLER role. C then calls safetySwitch() again - the check `_operatingController != _msgSender()` passes (C == C). Operations resume. C (with colluding minter) can now mint/burn despite role revocation.
prerequisites: Controller must have activated the safety switch
Controller role must have been revoked afterward

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Emergency halt can be circumvented, allowing unauthorized minting/burning to resume
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders if combined with compromised minter
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can pause the contract as alternative emergency mechanism

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for removed controller attempting to re-enable safety switch

threats.title: MASTER_MINTER With MINTER_ROLE Gets Unexpectedly Constrained by Minter Allowance
threats.hypothesis_description: In mint(), the allowance check applies to any caller with MINTER_ROLE regardless of also holding MASTER_MINTER. If an address holds both roles, the MINTER_ROLE allowance check is enforced, unexpectedly limiting a MASTER_MINTER who should have unlimited minting capability. The second if-block checks hasRole(MINTER_ROLE) without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a logic defect where the code contradicts the tiered minting design. MASTER_MINTER is intended to have unlimited minting authority, but holding both roles triggers allowance enforcement. This could block legitimate minting operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender())) applies allowance check without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders; should be: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()) && !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()))

preconditions: An address holds both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE simultaneously
The MINTER_ROLE allowance for that address is below the desired mint amount

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender())) { ... allowance check ... } applies regardless of MASTER_MINTER status
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: MASTER_MINTER's unlimited minting is suppressed if they also have MINTER_ROLE
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()) condition in the allowance check block

analysis_questions: Can addMinter be called with the MASTER_MINTER address as target?
Is there any scenario where MASTER_MINTER automatically receives MINTER_ROLE?
Does this affect the setMasterMinter flow?
validation_requirements: Test: grant both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE to same address, attempt mint exceeding allowance
Verify addMinter does not prevent adding an existing MASTER_MINTER
Check if setMasterMinter grants MINTER_ROLE alongside MASTER_MINTER

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: MASTER_MINTER address is also granted MINTER_ROLE via addMinter(masterMinterAddr, 1000). Master minter attempts mint(recipient, 5000). The access check passes (has MASTER_MINTER). But allowance check triggers (has MINTER_ROLE) and reverts because 5000 > 1000 allowance. Master minter is unexpectedly limited.
prerequisites: Same address must hold both MASTER_MINTER and MINTER_ROLE

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational disruption of minting capabilities for dual-role holders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol operations team managing token supply
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Removable by revoking MINTER_ROLE from the MASTER_MINTER address

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for dual-role (MASTER_MINTER + MINTER_ROLE) minting behavior

threats.title: Phantom Fee Requirement Blocks Transfers When feesFaucet Is address(0) But Fee Rate Is Non-Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: _payTxFee checks balanceOf(from) < txFees + txAmount and reverts if insufficient, but only transfers fees when _feesFaucet != address(0). When _feesFaucet is address(0) and _txfeeRate > 0, the balance check requires fee headroom that is never actually deducted, creating a phantom balance requirement that blocks transfers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script does not call setFeeFaucet. If ADMIN sets a fee rate but the feesFaucet is never set (remains address(0)), all transfers require balance >= amount + fees but no fees are collected. Users with exact balance for a transfer are denied service.
deployment_assumptions: _txfeeRate set to non-zero while _feesFaucet remains address(0)
threats.config_evidence: deployToken.js does not call setFeeFaucet() or setTxFeeRate(); both remain at default (0/address(0)). Risk materializes if ADMIN sets fee rate without first setting feesFaucet.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (balanceOf(from) < txFees + txAmount) revert; followed by if (_feesFaucet != address(0)) _update(...); Balance check always applies but fee deduction is conditional

preconditions: _txfeeRate > 0 (set by ADMIN)
_feesFaucet == address(0) (not configured or cleared)
User has balance >= amount but < amount + calculateTxFee(amount)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Balance sufficiency check includes fees that are never deducted when feesFaucet is zero
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Users locked out of transfers due to phantom fee requirement
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No early return or adjusted check when _feesFaucet == address(0)

analysis_questions: Is there any deployment step that atomically sets both feesFaucet and txFeeRate?
Can setFeeFaucet be called with address(0) to clear it while fee rate remains active?
How many users would be affected by the phantom balance requirement?
validation_requirements: Test transfer with feesFaucet=address(0) and txFeeRate>0
Verify _update is skipped but balance check still enforced
Confirm no fee is collected but transfer is blocked

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: ADMIN sets txFeeRate to 100 (1%) via setTxFeeRate but forgets to set feesFaucet. User with exactly 1000 EURF tries to transfer 1000 EURF. _payTxFee requires balance >= 1010 (1000 + 10 fee). Transfer reverts. The 10 EURF fee would never be deducted since feesFaucet is address(0), but the check still blocks the transfer.
prerequisites: Admin misconfiguration: fee rate without feesFaucet

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS on transfers for users with insufficient phantom headroom; tokens effectively frozen
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting transfers when misconfigured
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can fix by setting feesFaucet or clearing fee rate

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for fee behavior when feesFaucet is address(0)

threats.title: Forwarder verify() and execute() Have Inconsistent Validation Allowing Misleading Off-Chain Checks
threats.hypothesis_description: Forwarder.verify() does NOT check req.to == _eurfAddress, while execute() does. A request that passes verify() off-chain may fail execute() on-chain. This inconsistency can mislead relayers into submitting transactions that will revert, wasting gas without receiving the gasless base fee.
threats.investigation_rationale: Relayers use verify() to pre-validate requests before spending gas on execute(). The missing req.to check in verify() creates a false positive that leads to wasted gas when execute() reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: verify() checks nonce, sig, and selector but NOT req.to == _eurfAddress; execute() checks all four

preconditions: A ForwardRequest has req.to != _eurfAddress but valid signature and correct selector
Relayer uses verify() to pre-check before calling execute()

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: Forwarder.sol lines 60-72 (verify) missing req.to check present at line 87 (execute)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Asymmetric validation between verify() and execute()
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: verify() returns success for requests that execute() will revert on

analysis_questions: Do production relayers rely on verify() before calling execute()?
Can this be used to grief relayers into wasting significant gas?
Are there other validation differences between verify() and execute()?
validation_requirements: Test: construct request with wrong req.to, call verify (passes), call execute (reverts)
Quantify gas wasted per griefing attempt
Check if relayer fee (gaslessBasefee) is lost on revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker signs a valid ForwardRequest with req.to set to a non-EURF address. Relayer calls verify() which passes all checks. Relayer calls execute() which reverts at require(req.to == _eurfAddress). Relayer loses gas cost. The gaslessBasefee is never paid since execute() reverts entirely.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Relayer gas griefing; no token loss but operational cost to relayers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Relayers can add off-chain req.to check

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing verify() and execute() validation paths

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via repeated revert patterns from same address

threats.title: Unrestricted role-management entrypoints may allow any DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE holder to self-escalate into CONTROLLER/MINTER power without intended separation-of-duties
threats.hypothesis_description: Multiple role-management functions are externally callable and rely on AccessControl's internal onlyRole(getRoleAdmin(role)) inside grantRole/revokeRole. This can unintentionally make DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE (OWNER) the effective admin for CONTROLLER and MASTER_MINTER assignments (and possibly MINTER assignments), undermining intended tiering and allowing rapid escalation to mint/disable safety switch.
threats.investigation_rationale: Although this may be 'by design', code shows no explicit modifier checks on these entrypoints, so the effective policy depends entirely on role admin configuration. Because __ERC20ControlerMinter_init_unchained only sets MINTER_ROLE admin to MASTER_MINTER, but does not set role-admins for CONTROLLER/MASTER_MINTER, DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE likely controls them. Confirming the effective role-admin graph is critical because it defines who can mint, stop minting/burning, and manage minter allowances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external functions without explicit onlyRole modifiers calling grantRole/revokeRole directly (addController/removeController/setMasterMinter/addMinter/removeMinter)

preconditions: Attacker controls an address with DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE (OWNER) OR can obtain it via some other bug
Role admin relationships for CONTROLLER and MASTER_MINTER are default (i.e., DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) or otherwise permissive

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: role assignment/removal functions are externally callable without explicit access modifiers
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: actual authorization relies on AccessControl role-admin graph; may defeat intended separation (OWNER vs MASTER_MINTER vs CONTROLLER)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no explicit onlyRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) or onlyRole(MASTER_MINTER) on these wrapper functions

analysis_questions: What is getRoleAdmin(CONTROLLER) and getRoleAdmin(MASTER_MINTER) at runtime after initialize()?
Can DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE grant itself CONTROLLER and MASTER_MINTER trivially?
Is this consistent with documentation (tiered minting) or an unintended privilege escalation?
validation_requirements: Query role admin relationships on a deployed instance
Unit test that a DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE account can call addController/setMasterMinter/addMinter
Confirm intended governance separation in docs/spec

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE is expected NOT to directly control controllers/minters (separation-of-duties), a compromised OWNER could instantly add itself as CONTROLLER to disable/enable operating state, set itself MASTER_MINTER, then add minters or mint (directly via MASTER_MINTER path).
prerequisites: Access to a privileged key (DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) or a separate vulnerability granting it

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Material: enables direct supply manipulation (mint) and operational disruption (safety switch), potentially undermining stablecoin integrity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Limited; requires governance intervention/upgrade and potential blacklisting/clawback actions

threats.test_coverage_gap: No explicit tests shown validating role-admin graph matches intended policy.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: on-chain role events (RoleGranted/RoleRevoked) can reveal unauthorized escalations

threats.title: Forwarder allows arbitrary ETH forwarding to EURF token address via req.value, potentially enabling ETH griefing/loss and unexpected ETH custody semantics
threats.hypothesis_description: Forwarder.execute forwards `req.value` ETH along with the call to the token contract (req.to == _eurfAddress). Since EURFToken has no payable fallback/receive, any nonzero req.value likely reverts, causing execute() to revert and preventing relayers from executing otherwise-valid requests. Alternatively, if token later upgrades to accept ETH, Forwarder becomes an ETH forwarder into token unexpectedly.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even without a public mempool, attackers can craft requests that pass signature checks but deliberately revert (e.g., by setting req.value > 0 or req.gas too low), causing relayer losses/DoS. This is practical in production because it affects relayer economics and availability of gasless transfers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: low-level call includes `{value: req.value}` to req.to constrained to token address; token has no payable receive/fallback in Token.sol

preconditions: Relayer accepts and submits signed ForwardRequest without enforcing req.value == 0 off-chain
User/attacker can obtain a valid signature for a request including nonzero value OR can craft a request with valid signature from themselves and submit to a public relayer market

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: nonzero ETH value likely forces revert; relayer loses gas; if token later accepts ETH, ETH may be trapped or alter token economics
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no `require(req.value == 0)`; no explicit ETH refund handling

analysis_questions: Does EURFToken (current and planned upgrades) reject all ETH transfers?
Are relayers expected to enforce req.value == 0 and minimum req.gas?
Could execute() be used to launder ETH into token contract if upgraded later?
validation_requirements: Integration test: execute() with req.value>0 and valid sig; confirm revert path and gas griefing magnitude
Check Token.sol for payable fallbacks in current/next versions
Review relayer policy/docs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly submits meta-tx requests with `req.value=1` and valid signature (self-signed), causing relayers to waste gas on reverts and reducing gasless service availability.
prerequisites: Access to a relayer that executes arbitrary valid signatures
Attacker can fund signatures and submission fees (if any)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate: relayer gas losses; possible availability degradation of gasless transfers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Relayers primarily; users indirectly via reduced service
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High: add on-chain req.value==0 check or enforce relayer policy

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for req.value handling or ETH forwarding behavior.

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: estimate per-revert cost under typical req.gas values
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor Forwarder execute() reverts and req.value distribution

threats.title: Shared Nonce Space Between permit() and transferWithAuthorization() Causes Cross-Function Invalidation
threats.hypothesis_description: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() share the same _nonces mapping via _useNonce(). Executing one function consumes the nonce and permanently invalidates any pending signed authorization of the other function at the same nonce. A user who signs both a permit and a TWA at the same nonce loses whichever is executed second. Additionally, anyone holding a valid signed message of either type can execute it to consume the nonce, invalidating the other type.
threats.investigation_rationale: EIP-2612 (permit) and EIP-3009 (transferWithAuthorization) are designed with separate nonce domains. Merging them creates invalidation vectors that can cause failed transfers, missed payments, and user fund lockups when integrating with DeFi protocols.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Both permit() and transferWithAuthorization() call _useNonce(owner/holder) on the same _nonces mapping, consuming sequential nonces from a shared pool

preconditions: User signs both permit and TWA messages with the same nonce value
Both messages are distributed to different relayers or dApps

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _nonces[owner] is incremented by both permit() and transferWithAuthorization()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Execute a permit to consume nonce N, invalidating a pending TWA at nonce N
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack: prevent user's transfer from executing, potentially causing missed payments or failed DeFi interactions

economic_questions: Do any DeFi integrations rely on both permit and TWA for EURF?
Can an attacker profit from preventing specific transfers?
What is the cost of nonce desync for users?
validation_requirements: Confirm shared nonce pool in code
Test cross-function nonce consumption
Evaluate EIP-2612 and EIP-3009 compliance requirements

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 (only needs a previously obtained valid signed message)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; griefing causes failed transfers worth arbitrary amounts

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When victim has pending signed messages of both types
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could disrupt DeFi integrations relying on EURF meta-transactions

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who sign both permit and TWA messages
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Failed transfers, missed payment deadlines, stuck DeFi positions
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on value of invalidated authorizations

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm EIP-3009 specifies separate authorization nonces (bytes32, not sequential)

threats.title: Mint and Burn Operations Bypass Pause and Blacklist Enforcement
threats.hypothesis_description: mint() and burn() only check the _operating flag and role permissions. They do not call adminSanity() and do not check paused() or isBlacklisted(). Tokens can be minted to blacklisted (sanctioned) addresses and minting/burning continues during contract pause. The burn() NatSpec claims it 'Validates that sender is not blacklisted' but the code does not implement this check.
threats.investigation_rationale: For a regulated EUR stablecoin, the ability to mint to sanctioned addresses and mint/burn during emergency pause represents a critical compliance and security gap. The discrepancy between NatSpec documentation and actual code behavior suggests this is a code defect, not an intentional design choice.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mint() checks only MASTER_MINTER/MINTER_ROLE, amount > 0, and _operating; no call to adminSanity(), paused(), or isBlacklisted(). burn() NatSpec says 'Validates that sender is not blacklisted' but code has no blacklist check

preconditions: Caller has MINTER_ROLE or MASTER_MINTER
_operating is true
Contract may be paused or recipient may be blacklisted

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: mint() contains no reference to paused() or isBlacklisted() in its execution path
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Minter calls mint(blacklistedAddress, amount) or mints during pause state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Compliance violation: sanctioned entities receive newly minted tokens, creating regulatory and legal exposure

economic_questions: Is this intentional design (minting should bypass compliance controls)?
What is the regulatory impact of minting to sanctioned addresses?
Can a compromised minter exploit this during an emergency pause?
validation_requirements: Test minting to blacklisted address succeeds
Test minting while contract is paused succeeds
Verify burn() also bypasses blacklist despite NatSpec claim

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full minter allowance can be minted to sanctioned addresses
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% of minted amount reaches blacklisted recipient

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During emergency pause when admin expects all operations halted
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Regulatory/compliance impact; potential for unauthorized supply changes during emergency

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol reputation and all token holders via regulatory action
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Indirect: potential regulatory shutdown affects all holders
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire protocol if regulatory action is triggered

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare adminSanity checks in transfer vs mint/burn code paths
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify burn() NatSpec vs implementation discrepancy

threats.title: setOwner Can Leave Hidden Extra OWNER Role Holders Due to EnumerableSet Assumptions
threats.hypothesis_description: setOwner() performs grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) then revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)). If an OWNER previously called grantRole(OWNER, anotherAddress) directly (which is possible since OWNER is DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE's own admin), the EnumerableSet contains multiple members. setOwner only revokes index 0, potentially leaving additional OWNER-role holders hidden from the owner() view function, which only returns getRoleMember(OWNER, 0).
threats.investigation_rationale: The single-owner invariant is critical for upgrade security (UUPS) and overall governance. Hidden OWNER addresses could authorize malicious upgrades or role changes invisible to the protocol's nominal owner.

proof_of_signal.pattern: grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) adds to set, revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)) removes index 0 only; no check that getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1 before or after

preconditions: An OWNER has previously called grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, anotherAddress) directly
getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) > 1 at time of setOwner call

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: setOwner adds one member and removes one (index 0), net zero change in count if count > 1
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: OWNER grants role to hidden address, setOwner transfers ownership but hidden address retains OWNER role
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Hidden owner can authorize UUPS upgrade to malicious implementation, draining all token balances

economic_questions: Can getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) ever exceed 1 in normal operations?
Does the deployment script or initialization create multiple OWNER members?
What is the impact of a hidden OWNER performing unauthorized upgrades?
validation_requirements: Test: grant OWNER to address B, then call setOwner(C), verify B retains OWNER role
Verify owner() only shows index 0, hiding other OWNER holders
Confirm hidden OWNER can call _authorizeUpgrade

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full protocol TVL via malicious upgrade
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Two transactions: grant hidden owner + wait for setOwner

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete protocol compromise if hidden owner performs upgrade

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potential total loss of holdings via malicious upgrade

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check deployment script: deployToken.js calls setOwner(INITIAL_OWNER_ADDRESS) after initialize()
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify EnumerableSet behavior on add/remove with multiple members

threats.title: Fee Avoidance via Dust Transfer Splitting
threats.hypothesis_description: The fee calculation `(txAmount * _txfeeRate) / 10000` rounds down to zero for small amounts. An attacker can programmatically split a large transfer into multiple small dust transfers to completely bypass the transaction fee.
threats.investigation_rationale: Integer division truncation allows systematic fee evasion, depriving the protocol of intended revenue.
deployment_assumptions: _txfeeRate > 0
threats.config_evidence: Requires fee rate to be configured.

proof_of_signal.pattern: return (txAmount * _txfeeRate) / FEE_RATIO truncates to 0 for small amounts

preconditions: _txfeeRate > 0
Transfer amount * _txfeeRate < 10000

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Batching many small transfers in a single transaction or script
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker saves 100% of intended transfer fees

economic_questions: Is the gas cost of multiple transfers lower than the fee saved?
validation_requirements: Calculate gas cost vs fee savings on target chains (e.g., Polygon where gas is cheap)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare fee rate vs gas costs on Polygon

threats.title: Blacklisted Admin Can Evade Sanctions and Transfer Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: The `adminSanity()` function explicitly exempts callers with the `ADMIN` role from sender blacklist checks. If an admin account is compromised and subsequently blacklisted by the owner, the compromised admin can still call `transfer()` to move their tokens out, as the blacklist check is bypassed for admins.
threats.investigation_rationale: Blacklists should apply universally to outgoing transfers, especially for compromised privileged accounts, to prevent capital flight during an incident.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; flaw is in the contract logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!hasRole(ADMIN, _msgSender())) { ... if (isBlacklisted(from)) ... }

preconditions: Admin account is blacklisted
Admin has token balance

validation_requirements: Test transfer from a blacklisted admin account

threats.title: Missing Storage Gaps in Abstract Contracts Creates Upgrade Storage Collision Risk
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable, ERC20AdminUpgradeable, ERC20ControlerMinterUpgradeable, and FeesHandlerUpgradeable are upgradeable abstract contracts that define custom state variables but lack __gap storage arrays. Only Token.sol has uint256[49] __gap. If any of these intermediate contracts add new state variables during an upgrade, the storage layout shifts, causing collision with downstream contract storage. This can corrupt balances, roles, fee configurations, or any other state, potentially leading to total loss of funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: OpenZeppelin's upgradeable contract pattern requires __gap arrays in every contract that may be upgraded. Four custom abstract contracts omit them while defining custom state variables (_nonces, _blacklist, minterAllowed, _feesFaucet, etc.).
threats.config_evidence: Deployment uses UUPS pattern via hardhat-upgrades (deployToken.js). upgradeToken.js shows upgrade flow exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Defines _nonces, _PERMIT_TYPEHASH, _TWA_TYPEHASH, _trustedForwarder private state variables but no __gap array; same pattern in all 4 abstract contracts

preconditions: An upgrade is performed that adds new state variables to any of the four abstract contracts
hardhat-upgrades safety checks are bypassed or misconfigured

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Token.sol:__gap exists (line 163) but no intermediate contracts have gaps
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: OWNER controls upgrades; storage corruption is accidental or intentional
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any future upgrade that modifies intermediate contract storage layout

operational_questions: Does hardhat-upgrades catch this during upgrade validation?
Are there planned feature additions to these abstract contracts?
What is the exact storage slot layout across all inherited contracts?
validation_requirements: Run forge inspect storage-layout on EURFToken
Attempt upgrade adding a variable to ERC20AdminUpgradeable, verify hardhat-upgrades flags it
Map exact storage slots for all state variables

threats.title: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init Declared Public Instead of Internal Risks State Reset During Upgrades
threats.hypothesis_description: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init is declared as `public onlyInitializing` instead of the standard `internal onlyInitializing` pattern used by OpenZeppelin. While protected by onlyInitializing at runtime, this exposes the function to external calls during any initializing context (including upgrade reinitializers). If called during an upgrade, it would re-call __ERC20_init (resetting token name/symbol), __AccessControlEnumerable_init, and grant MASTER_MINTER to address(0), overwriting the legitimate MASTER_MINTER and disrupting the entire minter hierarchy.
threats.investigation_rationale: All other init functions in the codebase use `internal onlyInitializing`. This function uniquely uses `public onlyInitializing`, deviating from the pattern. The function body resets critical state: _setRoleAdmin(MINTER_ROLE, MASTER_MINTER), _grantRole(MASTER_MINTER, address(0)), and reinitializes ERC20 and AccessControl.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Function declared `public onlyInitializing` instead of `internal onlyInitializing`; calls __ERC20_init and grants MASTER_MINTER to address(0)

preconditions: Contract is in an initializing state (during upgrade with reinitializer)
External caller or malicious upgrade code invokes __ERC20ControlerMinter_init

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public visibility allows external invocation during initializing phase
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any upgrade that enters initializing state

operational_questions: Can this function be called during a reinitializer(N) context in upgrades?
Would calling it during upgrade overwrite the current MASTER_MINTER?
Does the onlyInitializing guard fully prevent post-deployment external calls?
validation_requirements: Attempt external call to __ERC20ControlerMinter_init post-deployment, verify revert
Simulate upgrade with reinitializer and attempt calling this function
Verify state reset effects if called during initialization

threats.title: Unrestricted setOwner() lets any caller seize DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE (upgrade/role takeover)
threats.hypothesis_description: EURFToken.setOwner() is publicly callable and does not enforce onlyRole(OWNER/DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE). It calls grantRole(OWNER,newOwner) and revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER,0)). Because grantRole/revokeRole are restricted to DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, the function will succeed only if the caller already has DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE; however, setOwner itself has no modifier and may be mistakenly assumed safe by operators. More critically, due to multiple inheritance and role initialization to address(0), there is a plausible misconfiguration path where DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE admin checks are not as intended, enabling an attacker to call setOwner and become owner, then upgrade via UUPS.
threats.investigation_rationale: Public owner-transfer functions without explicit access modifiers are high-risk in operations because they can be missed in reviews, and AccessControl admin relationships can be altered in upgrades or by unexpected role-admin configuration. This hypothesis demands confirmation of actual runtime access constraints and role-admin settings post-initialize.
deployment_assumptions: Role admin for DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE (or OWNER alias) is misconfigured (e.g., set to itself but membership set includes attacker or address(0) usable path), or a future upgrade changes role admins
OR a proxy/implementation mismatch leads to initialize/role state not being set as expected
threats.config_evidence: deployToken.js calls proxy.setOwner(owner) immediately after initialize(), implying setOwner is expected to work for deployer; but Token.sol setOwner() itself has no onlyRole guard, so correctness depends entirely on AccessControl role-admin state

proof_of_signal.pattern: setOwner(address) has no onlyRole; calls grantRole/revokeRole; _authorizeUpgrade guarded by onlyRole(OWNER)

preconditions: Some reachable on-chain state where caller can successfully invoke grantRole/revokeRole for OWNER despite not being intended owner (misconfigured getRoleAdmin(OWNER) or unintended role membership)
Token is deployed behind UUPS proxy and upgrade authority follows OWNER role as coded

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public setOwner with implicit reliance on AccessControl internals is fragile under upgrades and role-admin changes
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: May be exploitable without admin if role-admin configuration drifts
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after deployment if role-admin misconfiguration occurs

operational_questions: What is getRoleAdmin(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE/OWNER) at runtime, and can it be changed by OWNER/ADMIN?
Does the role membership set ever contain >1 owner (direct grantRole usage), and could that enable unintended callers to pass onlyRole checks elsewhere?
Do upgrades modify role admin relationships or AccessControl linearization/storage?
validation_requirements: On a fork, query getRoleAdmin for OWNER, ADMIN, MASTER_MINTER, CONTROLLER; verify only intended accounts can grant/revoke
Try calling setOwner from an unprivileged account on a deployed instance (should revert); if not, immediate critical

threats.title: Unprivileged fee-free token movements while paused/blacklisted via payGaslessBasefee bypass of adminSanity
threats.hypothesis_description: Token.payGaslessBasefee() transfers EURF using _update(payer,paymaster,basefee) without pause or blacklist checks. If any trustedForwarder exists (or is compromised later), it can move tokens from payers regardless of paused state or payer/paymaster blacklist status. Even without admin compromise, this creates an operational blind spot: basefee transfers may continue during pauses intended to halt all token movement.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete on-chain bypass of the token’s main safety controls (pause/blacklist) for a real value-moving pathway. It undermines incident response expectations and compliance guarantees.
deployment_assumptions: A trusted forwarder is set (non-zero) and can call payGaslessBasefee

The forwarder can be induced to call payGaslessBasefee in contexts that are not paired with a successful user-intended transfer (e.g., malicious forwarder implementation or alternative forwarder address set)
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script sets trustedForwarder; Token.sol allows ADMIN to setTrustedForwarder post-deploy, so exploitability depends on that operational configuration

proof_of_signal.pattern: payGaslessBasefee only checks isTrustedForwarder(msg.sender) and balance; then _update(payer,paymaster,_gaslessBasefee) with no pause/blacklist sanity

preconditions: trustedForwarder is configured to a contract that can call payGaslessBasefee
_gaslessBasefee > 0
payer holds at least _gaslessBasefee EURF

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Value movement path bypasses pause/blacklist controls
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires a trustedForwarder configured; further abuse may require forwarder compromise or misbehavior
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a trustedForwarder is live, including during pause events

operational_questions: Is payGaslessBasefee ever callable independently of a successful meta-tx transfer in the deployed forwarder?
Are paymasters blacklisted ever, and can they still receive basefees?
During incident response, do operators expect pause() to stop *all* token movements?

Check whether the Forwarder always calls payGaslessBasefee only after successful transfer (and cannot be made to call it otherwise)

threats.title: Relayer Griefing via Forwarder Inner Call Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: The `execute` function in the Forwarder requires the inner low-level call to succeed (`require(success)`). If a user signs a meta-transaction and then invalidates it (e.g., by transferring their balance so the inner transfer fails), the entire `execute` transaction reverts. The relayer pays gas but does not receive the `_gaslessBasefee`.
threats.investigation_rationale: Robust meta-transaction forwarders must isolate inner call failures from the outer execution to ensure relayers are compensated for validly submitted (but failing) transactions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(success) on inner low-level call

preconditions: User signs valid ForwardRequest
User alters state to make inner transfer fail before relayer executes

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should emit event on failure and still charge base fee, or not revert

analysis_questions: Can users systematically drain relayer gas?
Is the base fee high enough to cover revert gas?
validation_requirements: Test execute with failing inner call

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User signs a gasless transfer. Relayer submits it. User front-runs (or executes in prior block) a transfer emptying their wallet. Relayer's transaction reverts. Relayer loses gas.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Relayers lose gas funds, potentially halting the relayer network

threats.investigation_rationale: If OWNER ever calls grantRole(ADMIN, X) directly (creating multiple admins), subsequent setAdministrator calls become unpredictable. The wrong admin could be revoked while the intended target retains full powers.

threats.title: Post-Deployment Configuration Gap - ADMIN and MASTER_MINTER Not Initialized
threats.hypothesis_description: Token.initialize() grants ADMIN to address(0) and MASTER_MINTER to address(0). The deployment script only calls setOwner() but does not set ADMIN, MASTER_MINTER, CONTROLLER, trusted forwarder, or fee parameters. The protocol is non-functional for minting, pausing, blacklisting, or fee collection until OWNER completes manual setup.
threats.investigation_rationale: If OWNER does not immediately call setAdministrator after deployment, there is a window where no entity can pause the contract or blacklist addresses. If an incident occurs during this window, the emergency response mechanism is unavailable.

threats.title: Public Forwarder initializer allows hostile party to become owner and register permissive request types (if deployed uninitialized)
threats.hypothesis_description: Forwarder.initialize() is public and sets Ownable owner to msg.sender. If the Forwarder is deployed (or proxied) without atomically calling initialize, any external account can initialize it, seize ownership, and then use registerRequestType() to whitelist additional type hashes, potentially expanding what signed requests are accepted (including variants that bypass intended constraints in downstream tooling).
threats.investigation_rationale: Forwarder is a critical trust boundary for gasless transfers and fee charging. Ownership capture enables operational sabotage and could facilitate signature replay acceptance changes. This is a common real-world failure mode when upgradeable contracts are deployed without initialization in the same transaction.
deployment_assumptions: Forwarder is deployed as an upgradeable contract (proxy or implementation) and is not initialized atomically at deployment time
Token ADMIN later sets Token.setTrustedForwarder() to this Forwarder address (or it is already trusted)
threats.deployment_assumptions_note: If initialize is always executed atomically during deployment, this hypothesis is not exploitable.
threats.config_evidence: No Forwarder deployment/initialization script provided; only Token deploy/upgrade scripts are present.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function initialize(address) public initializer { __Ownable_init(_msgSender()); ... } and registerRequestType(...) external onlyOwner

preconditions: Forwarder contract address exists on-chain but has not been initialized yet
Attacker can call initialize() before legitimate operator does
Forwarder is (or becomes) the token’s trusted forwarder, or is used by integrators as an endorsed forwarder

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Forwarder ownership is established by the first initialize() caller; no constructor locks initialization.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: If Token ADMIN trusts this Forwarder, it becomes a privileged fee-charging caller into Token.payGaslessBasefee().
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From deployment until initialize is called (or if a proxy is deployed and left uninitialized).

operational_questions: Is Forwarder deployed via proxy or as a standalone instance, and is initialize executed atomically at deployment?
Is the Forwarder address currently set as trustedForwarder in the token?
Are there multiple Forwarders in use, and are any uninitialized?

Review deployment pipeline/runbooks for Forwarder initialization

threats.title: mint() and burn() Bypass Pause and Blacklist Checks Allowing Supply Manipulation During Emergency
threats.hypothesis_description: mint() and burn() only check _operating flag but NOT paused() state or blacklist status. When the contract is paused for an emergency, minters can still create and destroy tokens. Additionally, tokens can be minted to blacklisted/sanctioned addresses, violating compliance controls.
threats.investigation_rationale: For a regulated EUR stablecoin, the ability to mint to sanctioned addresses or manipulate supply during an emergency pause represents a critical compliance gap. The burn() NatSpec explicitly claims 'Validates that sender is not blacklisted' but no such check exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mint() checks hasRole + _operating + allowance but NOT paused() or isBlacklisted(to); burn() checks hasRole + _operating but NOT paused() or isBlacklisted(_msgSender())

preconditions: Minter or MASTER_MINTER role is compromised or colluding
Contract is paused and/or target address is blacklisted

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No call to adminSanity, no paused() check, no isBlacklisted() check in either function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Supply manipulation continues during emergency pause; blacklisted addresses can receive minted tokens
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing: whenNotPaused modifier and blacklist validation on to/sender

analysis_questions: Can a minter mint while contract is paused and then transfer once unpaused?

threats.title: setOwner May Revoke Wrong Address If Multiple OWNER Role Members Exist
threats.hypothesis_description: setOwner performs grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) then revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)). It assumes index 0 is always the current owner. If the OWNER role has multiple members (via direct grantRole call), setOwner revokes index 0 which may not be the caller, leaving additional hidden owners with full upgrade and admin authority.
threats.investigation_rationale: The single_owner invariant is critical for this UUPS-upgradeable stablecoin. Hidden owners can perform upgrades, role changes, and other critical operations. The EnumerableSet swap-on-removal behavior makes the ordering unpredictable after modifications.

proof_of_signal.pattern: grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) adds to set; revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)) assumes index 0 is the old owner; with >1 members, index 0 may be wrong after EnumerableSet swap

preconditions: Current OWNER has directly called grantRole(OWNER, anotherAddress) at some point
Multiple addresses hold OWNER role when setOwner is called

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Non-atomic role transfer relying on EnumerableSet index 0 assumption
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After grantRole adds newOwner, index 0 may still be old owner or may be a different member depending on set ordering
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1 before operation; no explicit revocation of caller

analysis_questions: Does the inherited grantRole prevent granting OWNER to additional addresses outside setOwner?
What is the exact EnumerableSet ordering after add + remove operations?
Can this be triggered by the deployment script's setOwner call?
validation_requirements: Test: grantRole(OWNER, B) directly, then setOwner(C), verify only C has OWNER
Trace EnumerableSet add/remove ordering with 3 members
Verify deployment: initialize grants OWNER to deployer, setOwner transfers to INITIAL_OWNER_ADDRESS

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner A directly calls grantRole(OWNER, B). Set=[A,B]. A calls setOwner(C). grantRole adds C: set=[A,B,C]. revokeRole(getRoleMember(0)) revokes A. Set=[C,B] (swap). B retains OWNER privileges invisibly. B can upgrade the contract implementation.
prerequisites: OWNER must first create multi-owner state via direct grantRole

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Hidden owner retains full protocol control including UUPS upgrade authority
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders if hidden owner upgrades to malicious implementation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Detectable via getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) check; fixable by revoking extra members

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for setOwner with >1 existing OWNER members

It does not check paused() state or blacklist status of payer or paymaster. A malicious or compromised trusted forwarder can extract tokens from any address (including blacklisted) even when the contract is paused.

If the trusted forwarder is compromised or if ADMIN sets a malicious address as trusted forwarder, this function becomes an unrestricted token extraction mechanism.

threats.title: Blacklisted ADMIN Can Bypass Sender Blacklist and Pause via adminSanity Exemption
threats.hypothesis_description: adminSanity() exempts ADMIN role holders from both pause check and sender blacklist check. A blacklisted admin can call transfer() and transferFrom() even when the contract is paused and they are blacklisted as sender. This means blacklisting an admin address does not prevent them from moving their own tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: If an ADMIN account is compromised, the owner might blacklist the admin and pause the contract as emergency measures. However, the compromised admin can still transfer tokens out because adminSanity exempts admins from both checks. The owner must revoke ADMIN role first, creating a race condition.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!hasRole(ADMIN, _msgSender())) { if (paused()) revert; if (isBlacklisted(from)) revert; } — ADMIN skips both pause AND sender blacklist

preconditions: ADMIN account is compromised
Owner blacklists the ADMIN address
ADMIN role is not yet revoked

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: ADMIN check bypasses entire block containing both pause and sender blacklist checks
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Blacklisting an admin has no effect on their ability to transfer as sender
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Blacklist should apply to all addresses regardless of role for outbound transfers

analysis_questions: Can the owner revoke ADMIN role before the admin can transfer tokens out?

threats.title: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init Has Public Visibility Instead of Internal
threats.hypothesis_description: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init is declared as 'public onlyInitializing' instead of 'internal onlyInitializing'. While protected by the onlyInitializing modifier, during the initialization phase it could be called by external actors to re-execute ERC20 init, AccessControlEnumerable init, and reset MASTER_MINTER to address(0) and _operating to true.

threats.title: FeesHandlerUpgradeable base setters are publicly callable in inheritors that forget to override, enabling permissionless fee parameter hijack

Because this code is in-scope and reusable, it is a practical 'footgun' that can lead to production incidents.

threats.title: Upgradeable storage-layout fragility due to multiple inheritance and missing __gap fields in several base modules may enable state corruption after upgrades
threats.hypothesis_description: Several upgradeable abstract contracts (ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable, ERC20AdminUpgradeable, ERC20ControlerMinterUpgradeable, FeesHandlerUpgradeable) define new storage but do not reserve storage gaps. EURFToken includes a __gap[49], but future upgrades that modify inheritance order or add state in any parent may corrupt critical state (roles, balances, nonces, forwarder address, fee parameters).
threats.investigation_rationale: While not directly exploitable by an unprivileged attacker, it is a practical production threat: a routine upgrade can accidentally brick transfers, mis-assign roles, or change fee/forwarder pointers—creating loss-of-funds, freeze, or privilege misconfiguration. This is especially relevant because the project includes an upgrade script (upgradeToken.js) and uses UUPS.

Owner/upgrade authority executes the upgrade in production

threats.title: Enumerable role-member indexing assumptions can break owner/admin rotation if role member sets contain multiple members
threats.hypothesis_description: setAdministrator() and setOwner() revoke the role from getRoleMember(role, 0), assuming a single member and stable ordering. If multiple members exist (e.g., due to direct grantRole usage), these functions may revoke the wrong member and leave an unintended privileged account active.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a correctness defect that can lead to persistent hidden privileged accounts and upgrade authority ambiguity. It is practical in managed deployments where operators might use AccessControl's grantRole directly (allowed for DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) and later use setOwner/setAdministrator expecting to rotate a single account.

preconditions: ADMIN role member count > 1 OR DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE member count > 1

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: role rotation uses index 0 of enumerable set

threats.title: Shared Nonce Between permit() and transferWithAuthorization() Enables Cross-Function Signature Invalidation
threats.hypothesis_description: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() share the same _nonces mapping. Executing one function for a given address increments the nonce, permanently invalidating any pending signed message for the other function at the same nonce value. An attacker holding a valid transferWithAuthorization signature (from a prior interaction) can execute it to consume the user's nonce, invalidating a concurrently submitted permit.
threats.investigation_rationale: Both functions call _useNonce(owner/holder) from the same _nonces mapping at ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable.sol. Standard EIP-2612 and EIP-3009 use separate nonce systems to prevent this interaction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _useNonce(owner) called by both permit (line 55) and transferWithAuthorization (line 75); same _nonces mapping

preconditions: User has signed both a permit and a transferWithAuthorization at the same nonce

threats.title: Shared nonce across permit and transferWithAuthorization enables deterministic invalidation/griefing (without mempool reliance via multi-sig workflows)
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable uses a single _nonces mapping for both permit() and transferWithAuthorization(). Any successful call to either consumes the next nonce, invalidating pre-signed messages of the other type at that nonce. In environments with off-chain signing queues (custody, batch signing, multi-party approval), an attacker who obtains any one valid signature can strategically submit it to invalidate other queued signatures.
threats.investigation_rationale: The chain has no public mempool, reducing classic front-run griefing, but operationally this still matters for custody/relayer systems where attackers may have one signature (e.g., previously issued authorization) and can later invalidate pending approvals/transfers that were signed in advance with expected nonce sequencing.
deployment_assumptions: Users or custodians pre-sign multiple permit/TWA operations assuming independent nonce spaces or stable nonce ordering

Attacker can obtain at least one valid signature from the victim for either permit or TWA (not via phishing; e.g., previously authorized action, leaked signature in logs, or legitimate but later misused)
threats.config_evidence: ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable builds both struct hashes using _useNonce(owner/holder), consuming the same counter.

proof_of_signal.pattern: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() both call _useNonce(...) on the same _nonces mapping

preconditions: Victim has pre-signed at least two messages (permit and/or TWA) expecting a particular nonce value
Attacker can submit one valid signed message before the other is executed
Victim/relayer system does not re-sync nonce before submission

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Single sequential nonce space couples two different authorization flows.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime pre-signed operations exist in off-chain queues.

operational_questions: Do relayers/custodians pre-sign and queue multiple EIP-712 actions for users?
Are signatures ever logged, stored, or shared between systems (increasing reuse risk)?
Is nonce re-synchronization performed before broadcast in the gasless pipeline?
validation_requirements: Model custody workflow with two pending signatures at same nonce and show invalidation
Review relayer implementation for nonce management and retries

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit it on-chain to consume the nonce.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim’s queued signature reverts due to nonce mismatch, causing operational DoS.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low once a signature is available.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Failed approvals/transfers, batch settlement failures, relayer retries and user support burden.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to medium; users must re-sign with updated nonce.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Gasless and custody workflows using permit/TWA.
